exits.label: ◂ back along the seawall to the Mortar

npcs.name: Vidkun Holt
npcs.title: the witch-finder who walked into his own Wall
npcs.hail: *(A big man is set deep in the Wall — deeper than the others, to the throat, as though he climbed in further on purpose and is still, by inches, climbing. He was a hard instrument once; you can see it in the breadth of the shoulders the stone is swallowing, the kind of frame built for dragging the unwilling. But the face above the stone is not a brute's. It is lucid, and ruined, and burning with a terrible clarity — the face of a man who has understood, exactly and completely and too late, what he was. He fixes you the moment your cold touches him, and there is something almost eager in it, the eagerness of a man who has been waiting a very long time for a judge.)* …*good.* …a *Returned.* …you *see* *true*, your *kind.* …then *look* at me, cold one, and *see* what i *am* — and don't you *dare* *flinch*, and don't you *dare* offer me a *hand* *out.* …i am *exactly* where i *belong.* …i *put* myself *here.* …and unlike the *innocents* i *fed* this *Wall*, i came with the one thing *none* of them *had.* …i came *guilty.*

npcs.name: Marenka
npcs.title: the last soul he condemned, who won't give him the hate he's earned
npcs.hail: *(A woman is walled a little way along from the big man, close enough to hear him, far enough that they have never once turned to face each other in all the time the stone has held them. There is no god's mark on her — Faithless, like so many he sent here — and her face has the worn, patient calm of someone who long ago finished the hard work of deciding how to feel, and now simply feels it. She watches you approach with a wry, tired warmth, and tips her head toward the big man down the Wall.)* …you've been *talking* to *him.* *(it isn't a question)* …everyone who comes *does.* he's got the *louder* *grief* — the *interesting* kind, the *guilty* kind. …*(a small dry breath)* …nobody ever *crosses* the last ten feet to *ask* the *woman* he *condemned* what *she* thinks. …*funny*, that. it's *almost* like even *here*, even *now*, the *loudest* *penitent* gets more *ear* than the *quiet* *wronged.*

conversations.title: Vidkun Holt — the penitent in the stone

variants.text: *(His eyes find the bare place where no god's mark sits on you, and something crosses his ruined face that is worse than shame — recognition.)* …*Faithless.* …*(he says it quietly, and you watch it cost him)* …you're one of *mine*, then. not *you*, not your *face* — but your *kind.* the *unclaimed.* the ones i *dragged.* …*(his jaw works)* …i sent a *hundred* souls *exactly* like you into this *stone*, cold one, *certain* — *certain* — that the *bare* place where their *god* should be was a *mark* of *sin* and not a *wound* the *world* *gave* them. …and now an *unclaimed* soul stands *free* in front of me, *real*, *whole*, *walking*, and i have to *look* at *what* i *fed* the *Wall.* …*good.* *look* back. …let the one i *wronged* be the one who *judges* me.

*(He marks the grey Doomguide's sigil on you and lets out a breath like a man greeting an old, hated reflection.)* …*Kelemvor's.* …*(a grim, mirthless almost-smile)* …i *wore* that *coat*, cold one. the *grey.* the *Judge's.* i did *every* *cruelty* of mine in *His* name and *thought* it *righteousness.* …*(harder)* …so *hear* me, brother in the *grey*: don't you *bring* me the *order's* *comfort.* don't you tell me *Kelemvor* *forgives* the *contrite.* i served that *forgiveness* like a *sword* and *fed* this *Wall* with it. …i *know* the *coat* you *wear* and i *know* every *lie* a man can *tell* himself while *wearing* it. …*judge* me as a man who wore it *too.* …and *better.*

*(He holds your gaze, and the eagerness in it is the unsettling thing — a man not dreading judgment but starving for it.)* …you don't *know* me. *good.* then you'll judge *clean*, with no *love* to *soften* it. …*(he draws what would be a breath)* …i was a *witch-finder*, cold one. the *Choir's* *hand.* the one who *came* in the *night* and *named* the *doubting* and the *godless* and *dragged* them, *screaming* or *silent*, to *this* *Wall* and *fed* them *in.* …*scores* of them. i kept *count* once. i *stopped* counting. …*that's* who you're *talking* to. …now *do* your *office*, *Returned*, and tell me what i *am.*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(He tells it without flinching, which is its own kind of penance — refusing himself the mercy of looking away.)* …i was *good* at it. that's the *part* that *damns* me, not the *zeal* — the *competence.* i could *read* a *bare* soul across a *room.* i told myself the *reading* was a *gift* from the *god.* …*(his voice is iron)* …and when i *died*, and stood at the *Judge's* *scale*, i *expected* my *Wall.* i'd *earned* it. instead they *weighed* me *faithful* — *commended* me — a *servant* of the *order*, bound for the *grey* *rest.* …*(and here, at last, something cracks)* …and i *couldn't.* i could not *take* a *rest* the souls i *dragged* were *denied.* so i *walked* — *past* the *scale*, *past* the *rest* i was *granted* — and i *climbed* into this *Wall* *myself.* the one *place* my *victims* *are.* …to *suffer* what i *dealt.* …it's the only *apology* …i *had* the *coin* to *pay.*

choices.text: *(read the witch-finder who sentenced himself — past the iron penance, to whether it's restitution or just the old cruelty turned inward)* "Hold still, Vidkun. Let me look at what you've actually built here. I want to see if it's an apology — or just the last sentence you handed down."

*(the one the Wall let back out, standing in front of the man who fed it)* "I am the one soul the Wall ever gave back, Vidkun. Which means I am the only one who can tell you what's on the other side of it — and what your suffering here actually reaches. Do you want to know? Or do you only want to be sentenced?"

*(the argument that unmakes his penance — that suffering-as-apology is the inquisitor's own creed, pain as justice, turned on himself)* "Here is what you've actually done, Vidkun. You built your whole life on one creed: that pain is justice. That the suffering of the guilty balances a cosmic book. And now you've turned that exact creed on yourself — your pain, here, paying down your debt. But it's the *same creed.* You're still the witch-finder. You're still feeding a soul to this Wall for justice's sake. The soul just happens to be yours. Your penance isn't the opposite of your crime. It's your crime's last, loneliest victim."

*(don't argue — just turn him toward the woman ten feet away who has been waiting a century to be heard)* "I'm not going to judge you, Vidkun. I'm going to do the one thing you've spent a hundred years refusing to do. Turn your head. *Look* down the Wall. Marenka is ten feet away — the last soul you condemned — and she has something to say to you, and you have never once let yourself hear it."

*(give him exactly what he asked for)* "You want a verdict, Vidkun? Here it is. You dragged scores of innocents to their unmaking, certain and competent and proud of the reading. You belong here. Stay. Suffer. The book is balanced."
"I'm not ready to rule on you, Vidkun. Let me walk the length a while."

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(He holds still under your sense, and you move past the iron penance to the architecture beneath it.)* *(And you find the remorse is real — there is no performance in it, no secret hope of being talked out of the stone; he believes, to his marrow, that he earned this. But your sense reads the shape of the belief and finds it is built of exactly the same stone as his crimes. The witch-finder's creed was simple: that suffering is the currency of justice, that a debt of sin is paid in a debt of pain, that the scale balances when the guilty hurt enough. And your sense reads that he has not *abandoned* that creed in his penance — he has *perfected* it. He found the one criminal he could sentence without doubt, himself, and is administering the exact punishment he spent his life administering to others, with the same certainty, the same competence, the same grim satisfaction of a book brought to balance. He thinks he has become the opposite of the witch-finder. He has become the witch-finder's masterpiece — its one perfectly just verdict. And under that, the thing he cannot see and your sense reads clear: that his suffering reaches no one. The souls he dragged are gone, dissolved or freed, beyond the touch of his pain; not one of them is one grain less wronged for his being in this stone. His penance is a private transaction between Vidkun and a creed, helping nobody, changing nothing, a man hurting himself in an empty room and calling the echo justice. And the deepest layer, the one that would break him: that real restitution would require the one thing his whole soul is built to refuse — to stop believing pain is justice at all. To get out. And to spend whatever he has left not suffering for the Wall, but unbuilding it.)*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(The question lands somewhere his armor doesn't cover, and for the first time the eagerness for judgment falters into something more honest: fear.)* …what's …on the *other* *side.* …*(he had not let himself want to know)* …i have spent — i don't *know* how long — *certain* that my *suffering* here *reaches* them. that every *hour* in this *stone* is an *hour* of *apology* the souls i *dragged* can somehow *feel.* …*(his voice drops)* …you're about to tell me it *doesn't.* aren't you. that they're *gone*, and my *pain* touches *nothing*, and i've been — *(he stops)* …*no.* don't. …*(and then, because under the witch-finder there was once a man)* …*do.* …*tell* me. i sent souls *screaming* into a *dark* i told them was *deserved.* the *least* i can do is *hear* the *truth* about the *dark* i chose. …*is* my *suffering* *worth* anything to *them*, Returned? …*any* of them?

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(You lay it in front of him — the creed, the mirror, the empty room — and you watch a man who armored himself in penance take the one wound penance can't deflect: the discovery that his atonement is his sin wearing a martyr's coat.)* …*the* *same* *creed.* …*(he says it slowly, and it goes through him like a blade through the gaps in plate)* …*pain* *as* *justice.* …i *built* my *life* on it, and i thought i *renounced* it by turning it on *myself*, and i — …*(he is silent a long, terrible moment)* …i never *renounced* it at all. i just found a *heretic* i could burn *forever* with a *clear* *conscience.* …*me.* …*(and then, the thing that costs him everything)* …then this stone is not my *apology.* it's my *last* *sermon.* …*(slowly, the grey beginning to loosen around his throat as a century of false penance cracks)* …if i *meant* it — if i *truly* meant to *answer* for the souls i *dragged* — i would not *sit* here *paying* a *Wall* that *eats* them *still.* i would *get* *out*, and i would spend whatever i *am* …*tearing* it *down.* one *stone.* one *soul.* one *night-raid* in *reverse.* …*(he looks at you, and the witch-finder's certainty is gone, replaced by something rawer and far more dangerous to the Choir)* …you didn't *forgive* me, Returned. you've no *standing* to, and you didn't *insult* me by *trying.* …you did *worse.* you gave me *work.* …*(the stone releases him, and he does not rest — he turns, at once, toward the length of the Wall, a hard instrument with a new and opposite purpose)* …*god* *help* the *Choir.* …i was *very* *good* at this. …and now i know *exactly* where they *keep* the *innocent.*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(You find the exact seam — not an accusation but a key — and it does not break him, it *re-forges* him. The argument lands so clean that the man does the rarest thing a true believer ever does: he changes creeds entirely, in a single breath, and brings all his old terrible competence with him to the new one.)* …*(he is utterly still, and then he laughs — a low, shaking, astonished sound)* …*a* *hundred* *years.* …a *hundred* *years* in this *stone*, and *no* one — not a *priest*, not a *god*, not my *own* *clever* *guilt* — ever *said* the *one* *true* thing: that my *penance* was my *crime* with the *blade* turned *inward.* …*(the grey sloughs off him in sheets, releasing a frame built for hard work)* …you want to know the *joke*, Returned? i am the *best* *weapon* the *Choir* ever *forged*, and they *spent* a *century* *congratulating* themselves that i'd *locked* myself *away* where i could do no more *harm.* …*(he flexes a hand the stone has just freed, and there is something terrible and magnificent in it)* …they should have *prayed* i *stayed* *guilty.* a *guilty* man *suffers.* …a *redeemed* one *hunts.* …*(he turns to the Wall, and for the first time in a century there is not penance in his face but *purpose*)* …i *know* their *signs.* i *know* their *night-doctrine*, their *quotas*, the *rooms* where they *keep* the *named* before the *dragging.* i *taught* half of it. …i am going to walk *out* of here, cold one, and i am going to become the *thing* the *Choir* *tells* its *finders* about to *frighten* them: the *one* who *turned.* …*(at the edge of the light, he looks back, and inclines his head — not gratitude, something heavier)* …you didn't *save* a *penitent.* you *recruited* a *witch-finder.* …*against* the *witch-finders.* …pray you *never* learn how *good* i *am.*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(The argument is sound but you reach for it too coldly, all logic and no hand, and a man drowning in a century of guilt cannot take a lifeline thrown like a verdict.)* …*clever.* …*(he shakes his head slowly)* …you're *clever*, Returned, and you might even be *right* — but you said it like a *prosecutor*, and i have *heard* enough *prosecutors*, i *was* one. …*(he sets his jaw)* …you can't *reason* a man out of a *grief* this *old* with a *syllogism.* the *logic* doesn't *reach* the *place* the *guilt* *lives.* …*(quieter, and the door is not shut)* …come *back* and try *again*, cold one — and next time, don't argue me out of *hell* like you're *winning* a *point.* …*talk* to me like there's a *man* in the *stone* and not just an *error* in a *proof.* …or — *(his eyes flick, just once, down the Wall toward the woman, then away)* …*no.* …never mind. …just …come *back.*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(He gathers himself, the iron settling back, but looser now, a hinge that has been shown it can move.)* …*(a long breath)* …you *rattled* something, i'll grant you *that.* …a *hundred* years i've been *certain* of the *shape* of my *penance*, and you've put a *crack* in the *certainty*, and the *draft* through it is *cold.* …*(he almost smiles)* …*go.* *think.* *walk* the *length.* …*(and, lower, almost despite himself)* …and if you *do* come *back* …you might *find* …that the *argument* you *want* …is not *yours* to *make* at *all.* …there's a *voice* on this *Wall* with *more* *standing* than *either* of us. …i've spent a *century* …*not* *turning* my *head* to *hear* it.

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(You overreach — make it too neat, too triumphant, *your penance is just your crime again, isn't that clever* — and the witch-finder, who has heard cleverness used as a blade his whole life, recognizes the tool and closes around his guilt like a fist.)* …*no.* …*(flat, final)* …i see what you're *doing.* you think if you can *out-argue* my *guilt* you can *tidy* me *out* of this *stone* and feel *merciful* for it. …*(cold)* …that's not *mercy*, Returned. that's *vanity* wearing *mercy's* *coat* — and i *know* that *coat*, i *wore* it the *whole* of my *life.* …my *guilt* is the *one* *true* thing i have *left*, the *only* part of me that *sees* *clearly*, and you'll *not* *talk* me out of the *one* clear *sight* i *own.* …*(he sets back into the stone, deeper)* …*go.* …and if you *ever* find a *thing* worth *saying* to a man like *me* …it *won't* be a *clever* one. …*(and, almost inaudible, the same flick of the eyes down the Wall)* …it *won't* even be *yours.*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(You don't argue. You just step aside, and point, and refuse to fill the silence — and a man who has spent a hundred years facing rigidly forward, away from the woman he condemned, finally, with a terrible effort, turns his head.)* …*(he looks down the Wall, and sees her — Marenka, the last one, the one whose face he has carried behind his eyes for a century and never once let himself actually look at — and she is already looking back, has perhaps been looking back for a hundred years, waiting)* …*(and Marenka speaks, and her voice is not the voice of the screaming he remembers, it is quiet and worn and unbearably kind)* …'i *don't* *hate* you, Vidkun.' …'i *did*, for a *while.* it *passed.* hate's *heavy* and i had *forever* to *carry* it and i *put* it *down.* …'i don't *blame* the *hand*, Vidkun. i *blame* the *Wall* that *made* a *good* man into a *hand.*' …*(and the witch-finder, who could withstand any verdict, any logic, any judge — who came here *starving* to be condemned — breaks completely, at last, on the one thing he has no armor against: not being condemned, but being *forgiven*, by the one soul with the standing to do it)* …*(he weeps, the great shoulders shaking the stone)* …*Marenka.* …*all* this *time.* …*ten* *feet.* …and i was too *proud* of my *guilt* …to *turn* my *head* …and let you *take* it *from* me. …*(and the grey loosens around them both)* …*(you have not judged him, cold one. you have done the harder thing. you made him* listen.*)*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(You give him the verdict he begged for, and you watch a terrible gratitude flood the ruined face — and underneath it, the faintest flicker of something that might be despair, that even now, condemned, he is not free.)* …*(he bows his head, and the stone takes another inch of him)* …*thank* you. …*(and he means it, and that is the worst of it)* …*you* *see* *true*, Returned. you didn't *flinch*, and you didn't *insult* me with a *mercy* i didn't *earn.* …*belong* here. *stay.* *suffer.* the *book* is *balanced.* …*(he settles deeper, almost at peace, the way only a man getting exactly what he believes he deserves can be)* …*(and ten feet down the Wall, unheard, a woman watches you confirm the one sentence she has spent a century trying to overturn, and she says nothing, because she has learned that the loudest penitent always gets the ear, and the quiet wronged are left holding the truth no one crossed the last ten feet to ask for.)*

nodes.speaker: Vidkun Holt
nodes.text: *(He inclines his head, and faces forward again, back into the rigid penance, the waiting for a judge who will say the word.)* …*walk*, then. …*weigh* it. i'm in no *hurry* — the *stone* and i have an *understanding.* …*(grim)* …but *cold* one. when you *come* *back* with your *verdict* …*have* the *spine* to *make* it a *real* one. *condemn* me, or *don't* — but don't *wander* off and leave a *man* *half*-*sentenced.* …i've *left* enough *souls* *half*-*sentenced* myself. …i know what it *does* to them.

variants.text: *(She marks the bare place on you where no god sits, and her tired smile warms by a degree, one of the unclaimed to another.)* …*ah.* one of *us.* …*Faithless.* …*(she says it without the weight the priests give it)* …then you *know* the *joke* of this *Wall* already, and i needn't *teach* it to you. …*(she nods down the length toward the big man)* …he *dragged* me here for *being* what *you* *are.* for the *bare* place where a *god* *isn't.* …and now you stand *free* in the same *bareness*, and he's *walled* to the *throat* for *putting* me here. …*(dryly)* …the *Wall* has a *sense* of *humor*, cold one. it's just not a *kind* one.

*(She watches you with that worn, patient warmth, a woman long past the urgent part of her grief.)* …you *crossed* the *ten* *feet.* …*(she sounds almost surprised)* …*most* don't. most *hear* his *thunder* and *forget* there's a *quieter* *soul* down the *Wall* who was *actually* *wronged.* …*(she settles)* …i'm *Marenka.* i *caught* *babies*, in *life* — a *midwife*, *godless*, *useful*, *harmless.* …and *he* — *(a tip of the head toward Vidkun)* …he *decided* the *bare* place in me was a *sin*, and *dragged* me *here.* …*ask*, if you've *questions.* i've had a *century* to *find* the *answers*, and *no* one to *give* them to.

nodes.speaker: Marenka
nodes.text: *(She talks easily, a soul who did her grieving long ago and came out the far side of it into something like peace.)* …everyone who comes *wants* to talk about *him.* the *witch-finder* who *repented*, the *guilty* man who *walled* *himself.* …*great* *story.* …*(a wry breath)* …and *no* one *asks* the *woman* he *put* here what *she* wants done about it. …so i'll *tell* you, since you *crossed* the *ten* *feet.* …i *don't* want him *punished.* …*(she lets that sit)* …i *wanted* it, *once* — *oh*, i *wanted* it *bad*, for the *first* *long* while. …but *hate's* a *weight*, cold one, and i had *forever* to *carry* it, and one *day* i looked at the *load* and i *put* it *down.* …not for *his* sake. for *mine.* …and *now* i watch *visitor* after *visitor* *confirm* his *sentence* …*helping* him *stay* …and i think: you're not giving him *justice.* you're giving him the *one* thing he *wants*, and *calling* it *justice*, the same way *he* *did* to *me.*

choices.text: *(read the woman who put the hate down — past the peace, to whether it cost her something to make it)* "Let me look at you, Marenka. At what it took to set that weight down. I want to see if the peace is whole."

*(the one who walks both ways)* "I'm the soul the Wall let back out, Marenka — I carry things between souls who can't reach each other. He's ten feet away and has never turned his head. If you have something to say to him, I can be the reason he finally hears it."
"Tell me what you'd say to him, Marenka — if he ever turned his head. The thing a century of visitors never carried the ten feet."
"Why him, Marenka? Why spend your peace trying to free the man who damned you?"
"I'll come back, Marenka. Let me speak with him first."

nodes.speaker: Marenka
nodes.text: *(She lets you look, unguarded, and your sense moves into the worn calm and finds what it cost to build.)* *(And you find the peace is real — not denial, not a wound papered over, but a thing genuinely earned across a century of hard interior labor. Your sense reads the long arc of it: the first years all rage, a clean justified fury at the man who ended her for nothing, fantasies of his suffering she is not ashamed of having had. And then the slow, deliberate, unglamorous work of a soul realizing that the hate was a second cage built inside the first, that Vidkun had taken her life and she was, by hating him, letting him take her death too, hour by hour, and that the only thing in all the grey still hers to decide was whether to keep paying him that rent. And your sense reads the precise, unsentimental shape of what she chose: not to forgive him because he deserved it — she is clear-eyed that he doesn't, that what he did was monstrous — but to stop letting his crime author her existence. She put the hate down the way you set down a stone you've carried too far: not because the stone got lighter, but because your arms were the only thing it was hurting anymore. And under the peace your sense finds the one unresolved ache, the splinter the century couldn't dissolve: that her forgiveness has never been *received.* She made it, whole and hard-won and real, and it has nowhere to go, because the man it is for has spent a hundred years too proud of his guilt to turn his head and take it. She is not waiting to forgive him. She forgave him long ago. She is waiting for him to let her.)*

nodes.speaker: Marenka
nodes.text: *(Something quickens in her tired face — not hope exactly, she is past easy hope, but a long-banked want suddenly given air.)* …you could make him *hear* me. …*(she breathes)* …i've *said* it to his *back* a *thousand* times, cold one. into a *century* of his *rigid* *shoulders.* he *never* turns. he *can't.* …because to *turn* and *hear* me say *i forgive you* would *un-make* the *one* thing holding him *up* — his *certainty* that he's *unforgivable.* …*(her voice is steady and fierce)* …so *carry* it, then, since you *carry* things. or *better* — don't carry it for me. *make* him *turn.* make him *look* at the *woman* he *put* here and *let* her *take* the *guilt* he's *hoarding* like it's *precious.* …it *isn't* *precious.* it's just *heavy.* …and i am *so* *tired* …of *watching* a *man* *drown* *ten* *feet* *away* …*clutching* a *stone* i'd *take* from him *gladly* …if he'd only *turn* his *fool* *head.*

nodes.speaker: Marenka
nodes.text: *(She is quiet a moment, composing a thing she has composed ten thousand times and never once delivered.)* …i'd say: *Vidkun.* …i *don't* *hate* you. …i *did*, and it *passed*, the way *weather* *passes.* …i don't *blame* the *hand.* i *blame* the *Wall* that *took* a *man* who might have been *good* and *made* him into a *hand* for its *cruelty.* …*(her eyes are wet and clear)* …you keep *saying* you *belong* here. you *don't.* the *Wall* *belongs* *nowhere* — and *neither*, then, does anyone it *holds*, *guilty* or *not.* …*staying* doesn't *pay* me *back*, Vidkun. it just gives the *Wall* *one* *more* *soul* and *calls* it *justice* — which is the *exact* *lie* you *served* your *whole* *life.* …*get* *out.* …and if you *truly* owe me — *(this is the part that breaks her)* …then *owe* me a *life* spent *tearing* this *thing* *down*, not a *death* spent *feeding* it. …*that's* what i'd *say.* …if a *man* would ever *cross* the *ten* *feet* …the *other* *way.*

nodes.speaker: Marenka
nodes.text: *(She considers the question seriously, the way she seems to consider everything — a midwife's practicality, applied to the soul.)* …*why* spend my *peace* on *him.* …*(she nods, as if it's fair)* …because the *Wall* wants me to *hate* him. …*(simply)* …that's the *whole* of it, cold one. the *Wall* runs on the *idea* that *suffering* *balances* *sin* — that the *guilty* *belong* in *pain* and the *wronged* should *want* them *there.* and as *long* as i *hate* *Vidkun*, as long as i *want* him *walled*, i am *agreeing* with the *Wall.* i am *one* more *voice* saying *yes*, *some* *souls* *deserve* the *dark.* …*(her jaw sets)* …and *once* you've said *that* — *once* you agree *some* deserve it — you've *built* the *Wall.* you've *handed* the *Choir* its *founding* *stone.* …i *won't.* …*freeing* him isn't *forgiveness* of *him.* it's *refusal* of *the Wall.* …it's the *only* *brick* i can *pull* …from *inside* the *thing.*

nodes.speaker: Marenka
nodes.text: *(She nods, and settles back into her patient calm, a woman who has waited a century and can wait a little more.)* …*go.* *talk* to him. …*(a wry, warm glance down the Wall)* …and cold one — when you *do* — *remember* there's *two* souls on this *length*, not *one.* …he's got the *louder* *grief.* …*(softly)* …but i'm the one who's *right.*
